did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a deep learning-based multimodal fusion model (MMF-Net) using a deep neural network with intra-modality self-attention and inter-modality cross-attention to classify skin lesions using smartphone-collected clinical images and metadata. The model was trained and evaluated on the PAD-UPES-20 dataset for skin cancer screening and diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and 'SPECIALTY SECTION'. It presents a novel deep learning model (MMF-Net) for skin lesion diagnosis, detailing the methods (multimodal fusion with attention mechanisms), experimental setup (PAD-UPES-20 dataset, five-fold cross-validation), and results (accuracy, balanced accuracy, AUC metrics). The paper reports original experimental findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A deep learning based multimodal fusion model for skin lesion diagnosis using smartphone collected clinical images and metadata
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based multimodal fusion model (MMF-Net) to classify skin lesions using smartphone-collected clinical images and metadata. The model utilized a deep neural network with intra-modality self-attention and inter-modality cross-attention to effectively combine image features and metadata features. It was trained and evaluated on the PAD-UPES-20 dataset, which included 2,298 images across six skin lesion categories. The evaluation employed five-fold cross-validation and compared the proposed method against state-of-the-art metadata fusion techniques. The model achieved an accuracy of 0.768 ± 0.022, balanced accuracy of 0.775 ± 0.022, and AUC of 0.947 ± 0.007, significantly outperforming other methods. The results demonstrate that integrating metadata via attention mechanisms improves diagnostic performance, suggesting the model's potential as a screening tool.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six classes in the PAD-UPES-20 dataset used for classification. 845 cases in the dataset. AUC = 0.93.

all_task_types.target: Six discrete skin lesion categories: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), Actinic Keratosis (ACK), Seborrheic Keratosis (SEK), Melanoma (MEL), and Nevus (NEV).
all_task_types.details: The model predicts the probability of a skin lesion belonging to one of six classes using a softmax layer. The task is explicitly described as classifying skin lesions and estimating the probability y of a skin lesion belonging to a certain class c. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, balanced accuracy, and AUC for pairwise combinations of the six classes.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset composed of patient data and clinical images collected from smartphones, obtained from the Dermatological and Surgical Assistance Program at the Federal University of Espírito Santo (UFES). It includes images and metadata (up to 21 clinical characteristics such as age, lesion location, Fitzpatrick skin type, etc.).

use.training: Used for training the deep learning model (MMF-Net) via five-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing via five-fold cross-validation.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver-operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed model against baseline and state-of-the-art methods (baseline feature concatenation, MetaNet, MetaBlock) on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.
To determine if continuous metadata variables were normally distributed prior to hypothesis testing.
To identify statistically significant differences in metadata attributes between different skin lesion types for normally distributed parameters.
To identify statistically significant differences in metadata attributes between different skin lesion types for non-normally distributed parameters.
To compare categorical metadata variables between different skin lesion types.
To compare the performance of different methods (non-parametric analysis) across the five folds.
To perform pairwise comparisons of the proposed method against other methods (No metadata, MetaBlock, MetaNet) to assess statistical significance of performance differences.
To determine statistical significance for the hypothesis tests (Friedman, Wilcoxon, t-test, Mann-Whitney, Chi-square).

hypothesis_testing.test: Wilcoxon pair test, Friedman test, Student t-test, Mann-Whitney U test, Chi-square test, Shapiro-Wilk test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in the performance of the proposed method compared to baseline methods (No metadata, MetaBlock, MetaNet) or between metadata attributes across different skin lesion types.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the performance of the proposed method compared to baseline methods or between metadata attributes across different skin lesion types.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The proposed method significantly outperformed the 'No metadata' baseline (P < 0.001 for ACC, BACC, and AUC) and other state-of-the-art methods MetaNet (P = 0.035) and MetaBlock (P = 0.028). Shapiro-Wilk test was used to determine normality for metadata variables before applying t-tests or Mann-Whitney U tests.

PAD-UFES-20.experiment: Five-fold cross-validation comparison of multimodal fusion methods

Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).notes: Most difficult to identify; lowest accuracy due to frequent misclassification as BCC

statistical_significance.notes: P < 0.05 considered statistically significant. Proposed method significantly outperformed all baselines.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: The proposed method achieved the best performance in ACC, BACC, and AUC. SCC was the most difficult class to identify. The model was trained using SGD optimizer with initial learning rate 0.001, reduced by factor of 0.1 if loss stopped decreasing for 10 epochs, for 100 epochs.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 used as the image encoder backbone (specific pretraining details not reported, but implied standard ImageNet pretraining for ResNet-50)

architecture_details.image_encoder: ResNet-50 CNN to extract image features (x_img)
architecture_details.meta_encoder: Multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to extract metadata features (x_meta)
architecture_details.intra_modality_self_attention: Multi-head self-attention applied to both image and metadata features separately to assign weights within each modality
architecture_details.inter_modality_cross_attention: Two-path cross-attention mechanism: one path uses image features as Query/Value and metadata as Key; the other uses metadata as Query/Value and image as Key, allowing bidirectional guidance
architecture_details.feature_combination: Concatenation of weighted image and metadata features after cross-attention
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layer followed by a softmax layer for six-class probability output

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced to 0.1 of current value if training loss stopped decreasing for 10 epochs
training_details.data_augmentation: Horizontal and vertical flipping, color jittering, Gaussian noise, random contrast

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Neural Network Algorithm
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: horizontal and vertical flipping, color jittering, Gaussian noise, random contrast
Metadata feature extraction: multi-layer perceptron (MLP) applied to clinical metadata
Image feature extraction: ResNet-50 CNN backbone used to extract image features

Attention mechanisms: intra-modality self-attention applied to image and metadata features separately to assign weights; inter-modality cross-attention used for bidirectional guidance between image and metadata features
conclusions: A deep learning model (MMF-Net) using smartphone-collected clinical images and metadata for skin lesion diagnosis was successfully developed.
Including metadata significantly improves model performance compared to using images alone (P < 0.001 for ACC, BACC, and AUC).
The proposed model outperformed state-of-the-art metadata fusion methods (MetaNet and MetaBlock) in accuracy, balanced accuracy, and AUC.
The combination of intra-modality self-attention and inter-modality cross-attention effectively fuses image and metadata features, allowing bidirectional guidance between modalities.
The model achieved an accuracy of 0.768 ± 0.022, balanced accuracy of 0.775 ± 0.022, and AUC of 0.947 ± 0.007.
Melanoma (MEL) was the easiest lesion type to identify (AUC = 0.98), while Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) was the most difficult (AUC = 0.87), often misclassified as Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).
The proposed model shows promising performance and has the potential to be integrated into smartphones as a screening tool for skin cancer.
External validation using data collected from different sites is needed before the model can be put into clinical practice.
The current study is limited by the dataset size and the need to collect data from more diseases in the future.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the authors explicitly state that 'External validation using data collected from different sites is needed before the model is put into clinical practice.'
The dataset (PAD-UFES-20) is limited to only six skin lesion types, which the authors acknowledge is 'relatively few compared to real world scenario.'
Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) was the most difficult class to identify (AUC = 0.87) and had the lowest accuracy due to frequent misclassification as Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).
The model was trained and tested on smartphone-collected images, which may differ in quality and characteristics from clinical dermoscopic images.
The study relies on a single public dataset (PAD-UFES-20) with 2,298 images, limiting the generalizability of the findings.
The performance metrics (Accuracy ~0.77, AUC ~0.95) indicate moderate diagnostic performance, suggesting the model is not yet ready for standalone clinical diagnosis without further improvement.
